title: Cooler and ice priority plan
summary: How to use limited cold storage for the foods and medicines that matter most.
content: A cooler is not a second refrigerator unless it is managed. Open it rarely, keep it shaded, group items by priority, and do not use it for casual drinks if medicines or essential foods need cold. Keep raw meat sealed away from ready-to-eat food. If temperature cannot be known, treat time and ice condition as warning signals. When in doubt about perishable food safety, discard instead of risking illness.
materials: Notebook. Context: “Cooler and ice priority plan”.
Pencil. Context: “Cooler and ice priority plan”.
Labels. Context: “Cooler and ice priority plan”.
Clean storage space. Context: “Cooler and ice priority plan”.
steps: Choose cooler priority: medicine, infant food, essential perishables, then comfort items.
Drain water only if it helps keep items dry and cold.
Review food safety before each meal.
warnings: Do not taste questionable food to test it.
Do not put loose raw meat above ready food.
common_mistakes: Treating Cooler and ice priority plan as finished after the first attempt instead of writing the next review time.
Moving people or supplies for Cooler and ice priority plan without checking vulnerable people, exits, and safe limits first.
Mixing clean and unsafe items during Cooler and ice priority plan because labels, dates, or responsible names were not written down.
alternatives: Run a smaller version of Cooler and ice priority plan with one responsible person, one helper, and one visible paper note.
Use a safer fallback location or method for Cooler and ice priority plan if the first choice becomes wet, crowded, contaminated, dark, unstable, or unsafe.
Pause Cooler and ice priority plan and verify the written record with a second person before moving scarce supplies.
failure_signs: People cannot explain who is responsible for Cooler and ice priority plan or when the next check happens.
Supplies connected to Cooler and ice priority plan are unlabelled, damp, contaminated, damaged, missing, or being used for the wrong purpose.
The routine ignores water, hygiene, food contact, and waste boundaries and starts creating new risk instead of reducing existing risk during “Cooler and ice priority plan”
when_not_to_use: Do not use Cooler and ice priority plan as a reason to delay evacuation, emergency calls, medical care, fire response, or official local instructions.
Do not use Cooler and ice priority plan when the location is structurally unsafe, chemically suspicious, smoky, flooded, violent, or otherwise immediately dangerous.
Do not improvise hazardous repairs, dosing, chemical handling, weapon use, or electrical/gas work under the label of Cooler and ice priority plan.
short_term: For the first hours of Cooler and ice priority plan, keep the action small, visible, and reversible: write the goal, assign one owner, protect vulnerable people, separate safe from unsafe supplies, and set a review time.
long_term: If Cooler and ice priority plan remains necessary for days or weeks, rotate roles, preserve written lessons, replace fragile supplies, and train another person so the group does not depend on one expert.
if_method_fails: If Cooler and ice priority plan fails, stop escalation, move to the safest fallback, record what failed, protect people first, and restart only with a simpler written plan.
environment_notes: Adapt Cooler and ice priority plan to the real environment: check water, hygiene, food contact, and waste boundaries, local weather, building condition, water access, light, noise, animals, neighbours, and safe exits before repeating the routine.

title: Safe food donation sorting
summary: A sorting table for donated or recovered food with labels, inspection, and refusal rules.
content: Food that arrives during a disruption may be helpful or dangerous. Sort by sealed shelf-stable, refrigerated, cooked, damaged packaging, unknown origin, special diet, infant food, and pet food. Do not accept swollen cans, leaking packages, food exposed to floodwater, or food with unclear handling when it requires cold storage. Keep a refusal pile away from the eating area so it cannot drift back into use.
materials: Clean storage space. Context: “Safe food donation sorting”.
steps: Separate special diet and infant food.
Label accepted food with date received.
Discard or isolate rejected food clearly.
warnings: Do not taste unknown food to check safety.
Do not distribute food with damaged or swollen packaging.
common_mistakes: Treating Safe food donation sorting as finished after the first attempt instead of writing the next review time.
Moving people or supplies for Safe food donation sorting without checking vulnerable people, exits, and safe limits first.
Mixing clean and unsafe items during Safe food donation sorting because labels, dates, or responsible names were not written down.
alternatives: Run a smaller version of Safe food donation sorting with one responsible person, one helper, and one visible paper note.

Pause Safe food donation sorting and verify the written record with a second person before moving scarce supplies.

The routine ignores water, hygiene, food contact, and waste boundaries and starts creating new risk instead of reducing existing risk during “Safe food donation sorting”
